summary: This guest post by Professor Nii Attoh-Okine argues that underrepresented minority students are conspicuously absent from the big data revolution, despite opportunities across multiple industries. Using his experience at major conferences like Strata and Hadoop, he observes that minority participation as both attendees and presenters is virtually nonexistent. He frames this as both a moral imperative and a practical necessity, noting that underrepresented minorities will soon constitute the majority of school-age children in the US. The post concludes with concrete suggestions for how the big data community can actively engage these students through mentorship, institutional partnerships, and conference inclusion.
key_points: Underrepresented minority students are virtually missing from big data conferences, seminars, and activities despite the field's rapid growth
Demographic shifts mean minorities will soon make up the majority of school-age children, representing an untapped pool of STEM talent
The responsibility falls on both sides—the big data community may not be reaching out, and minority students may not be reaching in
Concrete solutions include developing invested mentors, partnering with minority-serving institutions, and actively inviting underrepresented researchers to conferences
The talent exists within underrepresented groups; the community simply needs to make the effort to engage them
money_quotes: With all the opportunities, hype, and advancement, it is very clear that underrepresented minority students are virtually missing in the big data revolution.

At a recent Strata and Hadoop conference in New York, one of the premier big data events, it was very disappointing and even alarming that underrepresented minority students (participants and presenters) were virtually nonexistent.
Minority groups underrepresented in STEM fields will soon make up the majority of school-age children in the states.
This means that currently underrepresented minorities are a rich pool of STEM talent, if we figure out how to tap into it.
Please do make the effort to engage and include underrepresented minority students because there is so much talent within this group.
key_insight: The big data community is missing out on a growing pool of talent by failing to actively engage underrepresented minority students, and bridging this gap requires intentional outreach rather than passive inclusion.
